speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to go, Mr. Glucksman. The wife was in the house, and the daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was their reaction upon seeing you come into the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Scared. They saw me coming in with a gun.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they there while you were questioning--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were there, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did they say?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't know anything about it. They lied straight into your face.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the daughter claimed the same thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: The daughter didn't say a word.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know their names or the last name of the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: The last name, Bauer. That's, that's what I remember because I saw it on the door.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long, would you say, between the time you first came into the house and started asking questions?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there for about 10 minutes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and then you killed him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the reaction of the daughter and the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Like, they would expect something to happen to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why didn't you?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean kill the woman and the daughter? I didn't think I had the heart or anything like this. Because I was so full of hatred, I never thought of a woman or a daughter would be-- that I could do something like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you say anything to them after you killed him or did you just go?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Just went, and we went on looking for more.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you find more?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- we found very few men in any house that we went into. And we saw somebody, like, we thought he is too old. That he couldn't have done anything, maybe he was not even involved in anything. But Dachau, the whole town looked like-- like the whole town would be full of Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean it looked like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, you could tell that the whole town is military-like. Dachau was not the big city. It was a small town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Actually, Mr. Glucksman, we could go back a minute. You were in Flossenburg? From Flossenburg, you said to Gross-Rosen, and then from Gross-Rosen, you went to Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you arrived from Gross-Rosen to Dachau, first of all, how long a period of-- how big a distance?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was, like-- we were traveling the whole day in cattle trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you left Gross-Rosen, did you know where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- we knew-- they said the we were going to Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you heard of Dachau before?

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard of Dachau, yes. We heard of Dachau. We heard from all-- a lot of camps. We didn't hear anything about Auschwitz or Birkenau because we were away hundreds and hundreds of miles. You know, Auschwitz is in Poland, and Dachau is near Munich.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the procedure like when you arrived at Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first procedure that I arrived in Dachau, when they let us off from the trains, and we had to march in towards the camp. When we got through the gates, like I told you, we saw-- you looked around. You see bodies laying near a big building and it stunk. You see the smoke coming out of those big chimneys. So you know that it's a factor where they burn people there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you smell it when you got?

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrible. It smelled really, really bad. It smelled like bones when you burn bones. I don't know if you ever-- from a chicken even. Or rags, when you burn, it stinks. And so it stunk. And they had tables from the main gate, maybe the distance, like, from the door over there to the step over there, all lines of tables across with the different initials from names. Like, you know, that the-- like a little marker that says A, B, C, and so on. So everybody should know where to go, to what table.

speaker: interviewer
text: Based on your last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm. So people start going, lined up in lines towards the tables. And me, we didn't have any documents or any papers on us. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But when they said-- when we came they said, Jews to stay where you are. All different nationalities should go out. So the Jews had to-- you know, they had stay in that line by themselves. And I thought to myself, either way I'm going to be dead tomorrow if they're going to find out that I'm a Jew. I'm going to be tomorrow in the crematorium. So I took a chance. I didn't stay with the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I walked into the other lines.

speaker: interviewer
text: With what group?

speaker: interviewee
text: With non-Jews. Dachau had all different nationalities.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. So it's just the Jews on one side and anybody else on the other? OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dachau had even Germans in there. And you could recognize them by the uniforms with the numbers. Each nationality had their own marker before the number.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like, what kind of markers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like, the Jew would have a yellow star. Poland would have white and blue. German would have green, something else with it, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what-- in what kind of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the number.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, before the number. Like, just a mark, you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: A mark before the number. You know, you saw the mark, so you could recognize the nationality of the prisoner. And then the number would be the number of the prisoner. There was no name. If they called you, they called you 8467 or whatever, that you knew that that was you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I walked out towards the place with the Gentiles, I came over to the table where I was next. But meanwhile, I had my mind already made up, what name I should use. So I remembered another Polish boy that went to school together with me. His name was Baransky, Stanisla. It just, just came in to me, and that's what I used. When they asked me the name, I told them Stanisla Baransky.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was lucky by it because the next day I heard that the Jewish people, they were already finished. They were sent to the gas chamber and to burn at the crematorium after.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which people? The ones you'd come in with?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish, yeah. But still by the liberation, after the-- after the liberation, I was able to find a few Jewish prisoners that did the same thing that I did. And some of them were there in Dachau, that they brought them in a day before from another camp. That they had to fill up the quota there. But a lot of them, towards the end-- after the liberation, a lot of friends of mine that became friends, Jewish and non-Jewish, they died anyway because when the Americans came in, they were trying to be too good. They were trying to give whatever they had to eat, and some of it was so, so rich that the prisoners died of from it, after they didn't eat for so many years. And here they gave them milk and chocolate and all that other stuff that their stomachs couldn't take it and they died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Glucksman, while you were at Dachau, for those Jews who were not killed, in terms of daily life, how was daily life different for those of another nationality versus Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: The other nationalities there were mainly working inside the camp, which it was not too hard work. So they just had it so much easier. They had different kitchens. They gave them better food. They treated them different than they treated us-- completely different. They had-- they call it-- I forgot what they call this-- like, a PX in camp. They would get, every week, coupons to go into the PX. And for whatever the coupon they had, they could go in, and pick out whatever they wanted. We never had that, the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: But as someone who they thought was Polish is that-- you were able to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of work were you doing while you at Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us, every day, to Munich. Most of it to Munich to clean up after the planes bombarded the night before. The planes come in-- came in even during the day, a lot of times, to bombard the city. The city was such a mess that-- Munich is a big city. It's almost like-- compares to, you know, to New York. People couldn't find their way into the houses. That's how they were smashed. But at that time, it was like a holiday for us because no matter what, you could find some food. Either if it-- it was a dead dog or anything. It was food. We had-- we could find something to eat. Sometimes, in a bombarded house, you could find some bread, or find a potato. So it was good for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the reaction of the people who lived in the surrounding area when they saw you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't tell you because we hardly would get in contact with anybody around the surrounded area of the bombarding. And on top of it, we were guarded very, very close. The German SS, with the dogs, so you couldn't-- you couldn't get close to anybody. But the biggest fun was when they bombarded some warehouses, where they had food prepared for the Germans to towards the front lines. There we had the best time. There we had everything that we wanted. Even bombarded, we had cookies, chocolates, cigarettes, candies, everything you want to think of. You know, the magazine-- you know, the warehouses. That's supposed to be shipped out towards the front line for the front line soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of the three camps-- concentration camps that you were at, which one was the worst for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gross-Rosen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why's that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like I told you before, the atrocities that I saw in there with the children's clothes laid in the front of barracks. That was-- that hit it so hard, terrible, because we knew that those children were either gassed in Gross-Rosen or they were gassed and killed in other camps. And they brought in all the food-- all the clothes, with all the shoes, with all the glasses-- eyeglasses. Everything to that camp, and we saw, in the front of the line of the barracks, lined up, such a big, big mountains of it. That could make-- it made you sick looking at it.

speaker: interviewer
text: From that first day that you were taken from home and to the labor camp till the liberation that talk now how many years in total had you been a prisoner?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six years. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] From '39 until I was liberated in May 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after the liberation? Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- you know, when I became friends with the soldier that liberated me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jerome Klein. So I stayed in Dachau until he was shipped out to Munich, which it was, I think, two weeks later. He took me with him. When we got to Munich, he went through a place, not too far from where he was staying in the-- in the building. It was [NON-ENGLISH]. It was near Rotkreuzplatz. Went in-- he found out-- they found out, first, where the German SS live or used to live. He would go into the building. I would make him empty the building. Just take whatever you wear-- an extra pair of pants, an extra pair of shoes-- and leave everything else. And then he told me to put-- to get in there. He told the German, give up the keys, you cannot live here anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is something you said to the German or he did?

speaker: interviewee
text: He did.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. But you were with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm. I was afraid to be in the building by myself. I figured there could be somebody there that could come in at night and put a bullet through me. I got myself a gun, and I slept with it that first night. Next morning somebody knocks at the door. It was a woman that used to come in there to clean the house every day. I did let her in, and I told her here are my shoes. I want you to shine my shoes. I want you to make me something to eat for breakfast. You know, I gave them orders instead they're giving us orders.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she ask you who you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: She could tell. She could tell by the accent. I spoke German, but they could tell. They knew that there were a lot of prisoners in Munich now because a lot of them came in. They all did the same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did she do it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. She would even go down on the floor. They were afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in Munich?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, I lived in Munich until I came over to this country.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd say about two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of work were you doing while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No work. We wouldn't go to work for Germans after you come out of concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do for two years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Black marketing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anything you could put your hands on. Even the American Military did black marketing, too. So you did business. You know, they gave you something to sell. You sold it-- coffee, chocolate. Sometimes, I remember, they'd bring you ham-- whole hams wrapped up in, like, burlap, and you tried to sell it. And you buy for the money whatever you want. But to go to work for a German after being six years in a concentration camp, nobody would ever think of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were those two years that you spent in Munich good years for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I met my wife in a DP-- a displaced persons camp, which it was 300 kilometers from Munich. I went there because I knew I would find a lot of people that I knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. You know, after the liberation, you know, from all different camps, they would go out to DP camps. They had no place to stay. A lot of people arrived from being with the underground in Russia. So they had to go to a displaced persons camp because they had no other place to go to. And the joint distribution there would take care of them. So there was food from that, and if there were any children born, or little children, everything was taken care of.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you left Munich and came to America, did you at any point in time go back to Poland to your home town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nope.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never went back?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No reason.

speaker: interviewer
text: And have you ever been back since?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in what year did you-- was your original plan to come back to-- or not to come back but to go to America versus Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had-- yes, because I met Jerome.

speaker: interviewer
text: The person who liberated you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We became so friendly. Then, after he was shipped home from Germany, first thing, he wrote me a letter to ask me if I would like to come over to the United States. I told him-- I said now he's making a joke because I have no relative in this country. Somebody has to sponsor you. Send him back a letter. And he says, that's no joke. My parents were so happy to see me coming home alive from the war. I was the only son. They decided that if you want to come to the United States, they're going to make all the necessary papers for you, which they did. And they send me the papers. I went over with the papers to the HIAS in Germany, showed it to them, and says well, if you want to go to the United States, you're going to have to go through the consulate. You're going to have to go through medical things, and all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I sent back and I says, yes, I would like to come to the United States. But I think it's a joke that you're playing on me because there's no way for me to come to the United States. Worked out that it was no joke. Now, when you came here, did you come with the woman who eventually became your wife or did you come alone?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. She came over two years later on the same ship, which I have the document--

speaker: interviewer
text: That was a name of the ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was an old converted army ship. It took us 11 days to cross the ocean.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so--

speaker: interviewee
text: And that-- when I arrived in New York, the whole [NON-ENGLISH], you know, friends, everybody, they were waiting on the dock. They didn't know for who to look at, but they had a picture of me. So the--

speaker: interviewer
text: So who was there waiting for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: His family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. Jerome's family?

speaker: interviewee
text: And all their friends with Jerome. They took me off the ship right away after I was going through customs and all that. They took me to their house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they live?

speaker: interviewee
text: They lived in Brooklyn, on Utica Avenue. And for three months they wouldn't let me do a thing. They wouldn't let me go look for any work or anything. Just to have me-- give me, everyday, different party with their friends, with their families. Everybody would bring presents and everybody was looking for somebody, you know, to introduce you to somebody, you know. And right away a lot of woman came over and they says, oh, I have a daughter for you. And I says, I'm sorry, I have a girlfriend. And she may come over pretty soon. And so soon I made enough money after I took a job.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of job?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a factory. Soon, I had made enough money, I sent for my wife. She was my girlfriend, and she came over a year later. And after she was here awhile, we got married here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you wind up in Connecticut?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't like New York. I was not used to a big city, didn't know the language. So I found out about a friend of mine who was in concentration camp with me together, that he lives in New Haven. So I called him up. And I says that I am in New York. He invited me to come down. I come down on Sunday, get off the station, get out from the station, and I see that's more of a town that I would like-- that I'm used to. I went back the next day, Monday, took my suitcase, came down to New Haven.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of business have you been in?

speaker: interviewee
text: When?

speaker: interviewer
text: Since you-- well, since that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was working from the beginning in the tailor shop. Then, after that, I opened up my own shop.

speaker: interviewer
text: Here in New Haven?

speaker: interviewee
text: In New Haven. And I am very-- well, I mean, anybody in the street-- doesn't matter black, white, policemen, anybody-- you ask for Sidney, everybody knows me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Glucksman, I have just a few more questions before we end. But we will need to just change the tape before I do that.
